[An MCT Commentary]

Sara Duterte cannot stop talking. And every time she does, the country burns a little more.

In the span of a single week — from March 4 to March 10, 2026 — the Vice President has managed to deflect from her own impeachment, attack a whistleblower, offer dangerous foreign policy advice on live television, and stay conveniently silent while her own allies humiliated women in Congress. All while running for president.

Sara Duterte is manufacturing distractions because the walls are closing in — and she knows it. THE IMPEACHMENT SHE CAN'T ESCAPE

On March 4, 2026, the House Committee on Justice voted 54–1 to declare two impeachment complaints against Sara Duterte sufficient in substance. 

The charges are not small: misuse of ₱612.5 million in confidential funds, unexplained wealth, bribery, and threatening to have the sitting President of the Philippines killed.

She has 10 days to respond.

This is the second time the House has moved to impeach her. 

The first attempt in 2025 was killed by the Supreme Court on a technicality — the one-year bar rule. 

That constitutional escape hatch has now expired. There is no more hiding behind procedural loopholes. The case is back, it is stronger, and she knows it.

So what does Sara Duterte do when the evidence piles up? She talks louder about everyone else. "SELECTIVE OUTRAGE" — THE OLDEST TRICK IN THE PLAYBOOK

On the same day the House advanced her impeachment, Sara released a statement calling out the "deafening silence" of civil society groups, religious leaders, and lawmakers over allegations that 18 self-described former Marines delivered suitcases of cash — supposedly billions of pesos — to government officials, including President Marcos himself. She said anger over corruption "should not be selective."

Read that again.

A woman accused of stealing ₱612.5 million in confidential funds is demanding that other people be investigated too. 

She is not denying the charges. She is not presenting evidence of her innocence. She is pointing at other fires and screaming, "Why aren't you looking over there?"

This is deflection so transparent it would be embarrassing if it weren't so effective on her base. 

She praised the 18 ex-Marines not because she cares about truth — she praised them because their claims target the Marcos administration. 

The enemy of her enemy is her headline.

The irony is thick enough to choke on. 

She demands investigations into others while fighting tooth and nail to avoid her own. 

She demands transparency while her confidential fund spending remains buried under contested documents and denials. FILING PERJURY AGAINST THE BAGMAN — ON THE SAME DAY

Also on March 4 — yes, the same day the impeachment complaints were declared sufficient — Sara Duterte personally filed a 340-page perjury complaint against Ramil Madriaga at the Taguig City Prosecutor's Office.

Madriaga is the man who swore in an affidavit that he served as Sara's bagman, claiming he delivered large sums of confidential funds on her orders and that her 2022 campaign was bankrolled by drug money and POGO operators.

Sara says she has never met him. Never spoken to him. Never heard of him until his affidavit surfaced.

The timing of the perjury filing tells you everything. 

She did not file it when Madriaga's affidavit first became public in November 2025. 

She did not file it in December. She did not file it in January or February. 

She filed it on the exact day the House declared her impeachment complaints had substance. 

One attorney for Madriaga asked the only question that needs asking: "Why only now?"

And then there is this detail: Madriaga's legal team claims their client was offered between ₱500 million and ₱1 billion to keep his mouth shut. A fraternity brother allegedly approached him and said, "You don't need to recant, just don't testify."

If Madriaga is lying, a perjury case is warranted. But the timing of the filing — deployed like a press release grenade on the day of an impeachment vote — reeks of damage control, not justice. "JUST SEND AIRPLANES" — FOREIGN POLICY FROM THE BACK SEAT

While the Middle East burns — literally, with the US-Israel war against Iran entering its second week and airspace across the Gulf region closed — Sara Duterte saw an opportunity to play rescuer-in-chief.

Her suggestion: the government should simply dispatch aircraft to bring home distressed overseas Filipino workers.

That's it. Just send planes. Easy. Her father did it during COVID, she said, so what's the problem?

The problem, which any person with a functioning understanding of geopolitics would recognize, is that the airspace is closed. 

It is a combat zone. 

Over 110 flights have been canceled or diverted. Military aircraft in those skies would not be seen as rescue vehicles — they would be targets. 

Sending a C-130 into an active war zone does not save Filipinos. It endangers them.

Palace spokesperson Claire Castro responded plainly: "It is actually impossible to do that at the moment."

President Marcos himself pointed out in a Cabinet meeting that flying into closed Middle Eastern airspace could put Filipinos at greater risk. 

The Department of Migrant Workers has already repatriated 649 OFWs through seven commercial flights since March 5, using open corridors and exit routes that were identified through careful coordination — not reckless grandstanding.

Sara Duterte compared the current crisis to the COVID-19 pandemic, when her father arranged commercial flights for stranded Filipinos. 

That comparison is fucking idiotic. 

A pandemic does not involve anti-aircraft missiles. A virus does not close airspace. 

The logistics of evacuating workers from a country being bombed are fundamentally different from booking a Philippine Airlines charter.

But accuracy was never the point. The point was to look like she cares more than Marcos does. The point was a headline.

After hearing her suggest we fly planes into a closed war zone, the only thing I could think of was: tanga ba 'to? 🤷‍♂️ SILENT ON SUNTAY, SILENT ON WOMEN

While Sara was busy offering unsolicited military advice and crying about selective outrage, one of her own allies — Quezon City Representative Bong Suntay — made a statement during the impeachment hearing and made lewd remarks about actress Anne Curtis to defend her.

Suntay's argument was that thinking about something — like Sara fantasizing about having Marcos killed — cannot be an impeachable offense. 

His analogy: he once saw Anne Curtis at Shangri-La, found her beautiful, and "a desire arose within me." He said he should not be liable for what he imagined.

He said that on the floor of Congress. In a hearing about whether the Vice President threatened the President's life. In front of the country.

The response was immediate. Lawmakers demanded the remarks be stricken from the record. 

Women's groups condemned it. Curtis herself refused to accept Suntay's apology. 

Even Malacañang said the remarks may violate the Safe Spaces Act. 

Ferdinand Topacio, another Duterte ally, made it worse by blaming Curtis for posting "bikini pictures."

And Sara Duterte said absolutely nothing.

Representative Leila de Lima — in a privilege speech during an all-female session celebrating Women's Month — asked the question no one in Sara's camp wants to answer: "Why is the Vice President silent when her fellow women are being degraded by her own allies?"

De Lima's answer: "Is it because those who made these statements are her allies? Is it because their remarks were made in the context of defending her?"

That is the answer. Sara is silent because Suntay was defending her. 

She will not condemn misogyny if misogyny serves her interests. 

She will not stand for women's dignity if standing means losing a political foot soldier.

De Lima also noted that this is the same woman who remains silent on Chinese aggression in the West Philippine Sea, silent while the Chinese embassy disrespects Filipino lawmakers, silent while fishermen are harassed. 

The Duterte family slogan — "Tapang at Malasakit" — is a bumper sticker with no engine behind it. THE PATTERN IS THE POINT

This is who Sara Duterte is. This has always been who she is.

She announced her 2028 presidential bid on February 18, 2026 — days before new impeachment complaints were formally initiated. 

A political science professor from UP called it "an early calculation" designed to send a message to the House: back off, I'm running for president. 

The Makabayan bloc called it a gimmick meant to "strike fear upon congressmen."

She files perjury cases on the same day impeachment advances. 

She invokes her father's legacy every time she needs a shield. 

She talks about corruption only when the accused are her enemies.

She talks about OFWs only when she can embarrass the sitting government. 

She stays quiet when silence protects her allies.

Former Senator Antonio Trillanes, who has been one of the most consistent critics of the Duterte family for years, said it bluntly this week: Sara Duterte is unfit to be president. 

He cited what he called concerns about her mental fitness — the word he used was "krung-krung."

That may be harsh. But look at the record. 

This is a sitting vice president who publicly admitted to arranging the assassination of the President if she died. 

A DepEd secretary who spent ₱612.5 million in confidential funds with no verifiable paper trail. 

A "public servant" who resigned from the Department of Education — the institution responsible for the futures of millions of Filipino children — and walked away like it was a part-time job she got bored of.

And now she wants to be president? 

Tang ina, di ba? 🤷‍♂️ THE FIRE SHE'S REALLY TRYING TO PUT OUT

Every statement Sara Duterte has made this week — every press conference, every perjury filing, every suggestion about airplanes — has one purpose: to keep you from looking at the impeachment case.

The case that says she stole public money. The case that says she lied about her assets. The case that says she threatened to kill the President. The case that now has enough substance — by a vote of 54 to 1 — to move forward.

She is not putting out fires. She is starting new ones so you forget the house is already burning.

Don't forget.

Seventy-one gas stations. Out of eighty-seven inspected. That's the number the Department of Energy came out with on Monday — 71 stations caught violating fuel pricing rules in the middle of a crisis that's already crushing ordinary Filipinos.

Let that ratio sit with you for a second habang nagkakape ka. 

That's not a few bad actors. That's 82% of the ones they bothered to check.

And you know what the DOE did? Issued show-cause orders. Gave them 24 hours to explain themselves.

Pinagpapaliwanag.

As if there's any explanation for jacking diesel up by 54% in one day while jeepney drivers are still figuring out how to get through the week. The Receipts

A gas station in Pasig pushed diesel from ₱51.98 to ₱79.30 per liter. That's not a price adjustment. 

That's robbery with a receipt. 

Over in Fairview, Quezon City, another station moved diesel from ₱48.76 to ₱71 per liter — a 42.68% jump — and when asked, the manager blamed "increased purchase costs." 

A third one in North Fairview hiked from ₱49.75 to ₱72 per liter. The manager refused to comment.

In Echague, Isabela, sixteen stations got notices of violation in a single town. 

Sixteen. 

Mayor Faustino Dy ordered them to stop and explain within 24 hours, or face permit revocation and DOE and DOJ cases. 

In Tagum City, Davao del Norte, one station added ₱8.35 per liter to diesel before any authorized adjustment even kicked in.

And those are just the ones that got caught. There are 14,000 gas stations in this country. The DOE checked 87. Sino ang Totoong Nasasaktan

A jeepney driver burns 15 to 25 liters of diesel a day depending on the route. At a ₱20-per-liter hike, that's ₱300 to ₱500 in extra fuel cost — on top of the boundary fee, which runs ₱800 to ₱1,200 daily.

A driver who works 12 to 14 hours, fights traffic from 4am to sundown, might gross ₱800 to ₱1,200 before expenses. 

After fuel and boundary, some of them are netting less than the guy stacking shelves at a convenience store. 

That's a person who works longer than almost anyone in this country and takes home almost nothing.

And then there's the commuter. 

The factory worker. The construction laborer. The retail employee earning the Metro Manila minimum of ₱695 a day. 

They don't fill up at the pump — but they ride the jeepney, the tricycle, the UV Express. When fuel goes up, fares follow. 

That extra ₱20 or ₱30 per ride, twice a day, six days a week — that's another chunk carved out of ₱695. Before food. Before rent. Before your kid's school supplies.

The gas station owners hiking prices from air-conditioned offices don't know what either of those lives feel like. 

They've never had to choose between a full tank and a full stomach. They've never stood at a terminal at 5am wondering if today's earnings will cover the boundary. Ang Batas na Walang Ngipin

Senator Ping Lacson reminded everyone that premature price hikes violate Republic Act No. 8479, the Downstream Oil Industry Deregulation Act, and RA 7394, the Consumer Act of the Philippines. He told the public to report violations to the PNP and LGUs.

Good. Fine. Report them.

But here's the thing we don't talk about enough. RA 8479 is the same law that took away the government's ability to control fuel prices in the first place. It handed that to "market forces." 

And for 28 years, those market forces have meant one thing: prices go up fast, come down slow, and nobody can do anything because the law says the market decides.

The penalties under RA 8479? For refusing DOE orders — two years imprisonment and a fine of ₱250,000 to ₱500,000. For general violations of the Act — three months to one year and ₱50,000 to ₱300,000. 

The heavier penalties — three to seven years and ₱1 million to ₱2 million — only apply if you can prove cartelization or predatory pricing. Good luck proving that in court when the DOE itself says it doesn't regulate prices.

Energy Secretary Sharon Garin herself said it straight: "The DOE does not regulate prices due to the Oil Deregulation Law. We do not dictate the prices charged to consumers." 

So what exactly is the enforcement mechanism here? Show-cause orders? 24-hour deadlines to explain? Compliance certificates that can be revoked but almost never are?

Pinagpapaliwanag. And then what? Ang Tunay na Krisis

The Philippines imports nearly 90% of its crude oil from the Middle East. Iran closed the Strait of Hormuz, where 20% of the world's oil passes through. 

Brent crude is already approaching $80 per barrel and climbing. 

ING economists flagged the Philippines as among the "worst impacted" countries because our retail fuel prices are market-driven and our subsidies are almost nonexistent.

President Marcos said, "We are victims of a war that is not of our choosing." He's not wrong about that. 

But here's what he didn't say: we are also victims of a deregulated industry that treats a crisis as a business opportunity. 

The war didn't create the profiteering. The war gave profiteers an excuse.

The government ordered a four-day work week for its offices to save fuel. 

It told agencies to cut consumption by 10-20%. 

Meanwhile, Shell is staggering a ₱24.25 per liter diesel hike over three days. 

Petron is rolling out ₱19.20. Caltex, ₱17.50. Seaoil, ₱21.85. These are the legal increases. The ones on top of whatever the 71 violators already pocketed. Ang Magagawa Mo

I'm not going to sugarcoat this. The options are limited. But they're not zero.

- Report. Use the eGovPH app — go to the eReport tab, select "Gas Station Concerns," fill out the details. Screenshots. Timestamps. Receipts. Make them undeniable.

- Report to the PNP and your LGU. Lacson said it, the DOE confirmed it — these are the channels. Don't just post on Facebook. File it where it becomes a record.

- Demand names. The DOE flagged 71 stations but didn't publish a full list with station names and locations. We deserve to know exactly who is profiteering off our desperation. Push for that transparency.

- Push your congressman. RA 8479 needs amendment. Even Malacañang called for it back in 2022. Former Energy Secretary Alfonso Cusi himself said fuel price breakdowns should be public — and that oil companies fought to keep them hidden. Four years later, nothing has changed. Wala Akong Sagot

I don't have a clean ending for this. There's no inspiring takeaway. There's just a number — 71 out of 87 — and a country of 114 million people being told to conserve fuel while gas station owners who got caught cheating are given 24 hours to write an explanation letter.

Pinagpapaliwanag. 

Sana naman, pagkatapos ng paliwanag, may mangyari.

Sana. Sources:

ARAW-ARAW ANG PETSA DE PELIGRO

I've been staring at gas prices all week and I can't get this thought out of my head.

Petsa de peligro. We used to joke about it. A day or two before sweldo, you eat noodles, you stretch what's left, then payday hits and you breathe again. It was a punchline. Something you laugh about with coworkers.

I don't think it's funny anymore.

Because for a lot of Filipinos, petsa de peligro isn't a date. It's Tuesday. It's Thursday. It's the day after payday when you realize the salary already has names on it. Kuryente. Tubig. Utang. Bigas. Pamasahe. And somehow, by the second week, you're back to choosing between buying eggs or cooking oil because both isn't an option.

I need to be honest about something. I'm not living that life anymore. I used to. I remember counting coins for jeepney fare and doing math at the palengke like my life depended on it, because it did. I'm a stay-at-home dad now, a ghostwriter trying to find steady clients, and while things aren't perfect, I'm not the one skipping meals.

But I can't stop thinking about the people who still are. THE PART I CAN'T SHAKE

Half of Filipino families still call themselves poor. Around two out of ten went hungry at least once in the last three months. And I keep hearing the same advice recycled over and over: mag-budget, maghigpit ng sinturon, mag-ipon.

Sinong kinakausap nila?

Someone earning 400, 500 pesos a day in the provinces, stretching that across food, fare, bills, and kids? You want that person to "tighten their belt"? Anong belt? Wala nang butas yung sinturon na 'yun.

Then you open the news and every Tuesday there's a new fuel price hike. Gas up. Diesel up. Kerosene up. Because of the war in the Middle East, they say. Global oil prices. Shipping lanes. Geopolitics.

Fine. I get that part. We import almost everything. When something blows up in the Middle East, we feel it at the pump in Cabanatuan.

But here's what gets me: the Department of Energy is now warning gas stations about hoarding and premature price hikes. They're sending out inspectors. They're coordinating with police. You don't do that if everyone is playing fair.

So there's a war far away driving prices up, AND there are people right here using the chaos to squeeze more out of every liter. Yung gera, negosyo pa rin sa iba.

And who absorbs all of it? The jeepney driver whose 12-hour shift now barely covers diesel. The factory worker choosing between riding or eating. The nanay doing palengke math at 6am, already knowing the numbers won't work. NINAKAW PA YUNG DAPAT SANA TUMUTULONG

This is the part where I feel genuinely angry. Not internet-angry. The kind that sits in your chest.

While ordinary Filipinos are being told to sacrifice, to understand, to adjust, we find out that hundreds of billions in flood control money went to a handful of contractors. Ghost projects. Substandard dikes that collapse when it rains hard. Infrastructure that exists on paper and nowhere else.

Then when typhoons hit and people die, the conversation becomes: "Bakit kasi nagtayo diyan?" "Bakit hindi nag-evacuate?"

Nobody asks: "Bakit kasi ninakaw yung pondo na dapat nandun para protektahan sila?"

We slid further down the global corruption index. Growth forecasts got cut. Investors are pulling back. And inside government, the response is still "we're looking into it" and "these are isolated."

Hindi isolated ang gutom. Hindi isolated ang baha. Hindi isolated ang pamasaheng tumaas na naman. TAPOS SINO YUNG GUSTONG MAMUNO?

And while all this is happening, 2028 is creeping in.

I see names being floated for president, and ang una kong tanong: ano ba ginawa nito? What has this person actually managed? What do they know about fiscal policy, about agriculture, about the price of a kilo of galunggong?

Because our presidential qualifications are basically: be 40, be Filipino, be a voter, be able to read. That's the bar. I've seen harder screening processes for call center applicants.

And the commentary around the race is already about "winnability" and "name recall" and "social media following." Parang talent show audition, hindi pamumuno ng bansa.

So we tell ordinary people: mag-aral ka pa, mag-upskill ka, pagbutihin mo sarili mo. But the people who want to run the country? Sapat na yung apelyido at TikTok account.

I don't know what to do with that. I really don't. Except sit here and feel the weight of it and write it down because keeping it inside feels worse.

Petsa de peligro was supposed to be a joke. A day or two before sweldo.

Hindi na siya nakakatawa.

Three million barrels per day. Gone.

Iraq, the second-largest producer in OPEC, has seen crude output from its main southern oilfields fall from 4.3 million barrels per day to just 1.3 million, according to Reuters, citing three industry sources on Sunday, March 8.

That is a 70 percent collapse in the span of ten days.

The oil fields are intact. The wells work. Iraq has nowhere to send what it pumps. STORAGE IS FULL. EXPORTS HAVE STOPPED.

An official from Iraq's state-run Basra Oil Company told Reuters that crude storage has reached maximum capacity. Whatever production remains is being redirected entirely to domestic refineries.

In February, Iraq exported 3.334 million barrels per day from its southern terminals. By Sunday, that figure had cratered to roughly 800,000 barrels per day, according to Reuters. Only two tankers, the Hong Kong-flagged Cospearl Lake and the China-flagged Yuan Hua Hu, managed to load. Each took on approximately 2 million barrels.

After those ships finished loading around 8 p.m. local time, no new vessels arrived. Three oil officials and a shipping agent confirmed to Reuters that export flows from southern terminals had completely halted.

A senior Iraqi oil ministry official called it "the most significant operational challenge Iraq has encountered in over two decades." THE STRAIT OF HORMUZ CHOKEPOINT

Every barrel of Iraqi crude from the south must pass through the Strait of Hormuz, the waterway between Iran and Oman that carries approximately 20 percent of the world's oil and liquefied natural gas, according to Reuters.

Since the United States and Israel launched military operations against Iran on February 28, the strait has been effectively closed. Iran's Revolutionary Guards warned they would target any vessel attempting passage. Tanker traffic fell from 37 vessels on February 27 to zero, according to Reuters.

Bloomberg reported on March 3 that Iraq had already begun shutting down its Rumaila field, the country's largest, along with the West Qurna 2 project. By March 8, Bloomberg estimated the production drop at roughly 60 percent. Hours later, Reuters confirmed the situation had worsened to 70 percent. UAE, KUWAIT, AND QATAR ARE NEXT

Bloomberg reported on March 8 that the United Arab Emirates and Kuwait have also started reducing output as their own storage fills. Kuwait's petroleum corporation confirmed "precautionary reductions" tied to "Iranian threats against safe passage of vessels through the Strait of Hormuz," according to CNBC.

JPMorgan estimated that actual production cuts across the region reached approximately 2 million barrels per day and warned that cuts could exceed 4 million barrels per day by March 13, according to TradingKey.

Qatar's Energy Minister Saad al-Kaabi told the Financial Times that all Gulf oil-producing nations could cease production entirely within weeks if the blockade persists, potentially pushing prices to $150 per barrel. WHAT THIS MEANS FOR THE PHILIPPINES

According to MUFG Research, 95 percent of the crude oil the Philippines imports comes from the Middle East. BusinessWorld, citing Department of Energy data, places the figure at roughly 98 percent.

Nearly all of the Philippines' imported crude passes through or originates from the zone now under blockade.

Brent crude breached $111 per barrel on Sunday. West Texas Intermediate climbed past $114. U.S. crude surged approximately 35 percent last week, the largest single-week gain in futures trading history since 1983, according to CNBC.

For Filipino consumers, the pipeline from Iraq's collapse to the gas station is direct.

On March 10, Philippine oil companies will begin implementing the largest fuel price increases in the country's history. Diesel is set to rise by P17 to P24 per liter across companies, gasoline by P7 to P13 per liter, and kerosene by P32 to P38.50 per liter, according to the Department of Energy and multiple Philippine outlets.

MUFG Research estimated that every $10 per barrel increase in oil prices widens the Philippines' current account deficit by 0.4 to 0.5 percent of GDP. At $100 per barrel sustained, GDP growth could fall to 3.7 percent in 2026. At $130, it could drop below 3.4 percent.

Around 2.1 million Filipinos live across the Middle East. The Department of Migrant Workers has declared the Strait of Hormuz and the Persian Gulf a "War Risk Operations Area," giving Filipino seafarers the legal right to refuse boarding vessels transiting the zone. THE CLOCK

Iraq relies on oil revenue for over 90 percent of its national income, according to Reuters. With exports halted and production gutted, the fiscal pressure on Baghdad is acute.

Goldman Sachs warned on March 6 that if Strait flows remain sluggish through March, oil prices could surpass their historical peaks from 2008 and 2022.

The wells in Basra are still there. The oil is still underground. The refineries still work. It is the passage out that is gone. SOURCES

🟥 P7 BILLION SHORT: THE REPATRIATION MATH FOR 1,979 OFWS AS THE MIDDLE EAST BURNS

Seven billion pesos.

That is the gap between what the Philippine government has budgeted for OFW repatriation and what it will actually cost if the Middle East conflict forces a full-scale pullout, according to Senator Sherwin Gatchalian.

In an interview on Super Radyo dzBB on Sunday, Gatchalian laid out the arithmetic. The 2026 national budget allocates P6 billion for repatriation. But worst-case estimates, including undocumented Filipino workers, push the actual need to P13 billion, according to GMA News.

The Department of Migrant Workers had already flagged the shortfall, saying it would need a supplemental budget to cover a worst-case scenario. The Overseas Workers Welfare Administration said repatriation costs per individual, initially pegged at P135,000 to P140,000, could reach P150,000 after the escalation, according to GMA News.

🟥 WHO IS ASKING TO COME HOME

As of March 5, 1,979 OFWs across nine countries in the Middle East had formally requested repatriation, according to the Department of Migrant Workers.

Kuwait accounted for the largest number at 633, according to GMA News. The rest were distributed across Bahrain, Abu Dhabi, Dubai, Qatar, Israel, and other posts in the region.

These are the ones who have come forward. The Department of Foreign Affairs counts more than 2.4 million Filipinos residing across the Middle East. Not all of them want to leave. But the number asking is growing.

🟥 THE ONES ALREADY MOVING

Repatriations are happening in waves.

On Saturday, 81 OFWs arrived back in the Philippines. On Sunday, 61 more landed at NAIA Terminal 3 from Dubai aboard Emirates Flight EK 334, including three minors, according to GMA News. They received financial and transportation assistance, temporary accommodation, and psychosocial support on arrival.

Separately, 11 Filipinos were evacuated from Iran to Van, Turkey, through the coordinated efforts of the Philippine Embassies in Tehran and Ankara, according to Inquirer. The evacuation was funded by the DFA's Assistance-to-Nationals Fund. Those 11 are scheduled to fly to the Philippines in the coming days.

An estimated 800 Filipinos remain in Iran.

🟥 ONE WOUNDED, ONE DEAD

One Filipino national in Doha, Qatar, sustained minor injuries from intercepted missile shrapnel, according to the Philippine Embassy in Doha. The individual is safe and recovering, according to GMA News.

The conflict has already claimed one Filipino life. Mary Ann Velasquez de Vera, a caregiver from Pangasinan working in Israel since 2019, died after being struck by shrapnel from an Iranian missile during the February 28 attacks on Tel Aviv. She was accompanying her elderly ward to a bomb shelter when the strike occurred, according to GMA News and the Philippine News Agency.

The Embassy of Israel in the Philippines formally mourned her death and recognized her service.

🟥 THE SEA ROUTES ARE NOW WAR ZONES

On Saturday, DMW Secretary Hans Leo Cacdac signed DMW Advisory No. 11, Series of 2026, classifying the Persian Gulf, the Strait of Hormuz, and the Gulf of Oman as "Warlike Operations Areas," according to Inquirer and ABS-CBN.

Under the advisory, Filipino seafarers can refuse to sail through these waters.

"Filipino crew members, especially ship owners employing Filipino personnel, should avoid operating in that region. If they must go there, they must grant Filipino crew members the autonomy to decline sailing and steer clear of these dangerous waters," Cacdac said in a video message Saturday night, according to Inquirer.

The Philippines supplies a significant share of the global seafarer workforce. The advisory sends a signal well beyond Filipino sailors.

🟥 CONGRESS STEPPING IN

House Majority Leader Ferdinand Alexander "Sandro" Marcos announced Sunday that Speaker Faustino Dy III has requested a joint briefing by the House Committees on Foreign Affairs and Overseas Workers Affairs, set for Wednesday, March 11, according to GMA News and Inquirer.

The briefing will review the conditions faced by OFWs in the conflict zone and the preparedness of government agencies to intervene.

"The House wants to receive a full briefing on the situation on the ground and on the readiness of our agencies to assist our nationals should conditions require government intervention," Marcos said, according to GMA News.

The DFA and DMW are expected to present the number of affected OFWs, security conditions, and contingency plans.

The Senate had already held similar hearings on March 4 and 5, according to Inquirer.

🟥 THE BIGGER PICTURE

The P7 billion gap is a budget problem. But the scale behind it is a human one.

Fuel prices are set to spike further. Minimum estimated increases for next week: P19 per liter on diesel, P9 on gasoline, P31 on kerosene, according to GMA News. If global oil averages above US$80 per barrel for three consecutive months, excise taxes on petroleum are automatically suspended under the TRAIN Law, costing the government an estimated P15 billion per month in lost revenue.

Repatriation costs, the oil shock, and the seafarer advisory are three threads of the same crisis. Together, they compound.

And 1,979 Filipinos are still waiting.
